name: Wu Xing
shortDescription: Wu Xing, often translated as the Five Elements, Five Phases, or Five Agents, is a Chinese philosophical concept describing the fundamental phases of change and interaction in the cosmos.
description: Wu Xing is a core concept in Chinese philosophy, cosmology, and traditional Chinese medicine. It represents five dynamic, interconnected phases or elements: Wood (木 mù), Fire (火 huǒ), Earth (土 tǔ), Metal (金 jīn), and Water (水 shuǐ). These elements are not seen as static substances but rather as constantly changing qualities and energies that influence and are influenced by one another. The Wu Xing theory outlines two primary cycles: the Generating (生 shēng) cycle, where one element gives rise to the next (Wood fuels Fire, Fire creates Ash/Earth, Earth bears Metal, Metal collects Water, Water nourishes Wood), and the Overcoming (克 kè) cycle, where one element controls or restrains another (Wood parts Earth, Earth dams Water, Water extinguishes Fire, Fire melts Metal, Metal chops Wood). These cycles describe the dynamic balance and constant flux within the universe and are applied to various aspects of life, including health, relationships, and governance. The concept is deeply intertwined with Yin and Yang, representing the complementary and opposing forces that drive change within the Wu Xing framework. Understanding Wu Xing is crucial for comprehending Chinese thought and its applications in diverse fields.

sources.title: The Huainanzi: A Guide to the Theory and Practice of Government in Early Han China
sources.author: John S. Major
